coredata.dc:title: Efficient electrocatalytic water oxidation by using the hierarchical 1D/2D structural nanohybrid of CoCu-based zeolitic imidazolate framework nanosheets and graphdiyne nanowires
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Jing
Liu, Jiameng
Wang, Changbao
Rong, Feilong
He, Linghao
Song, Yingpan
Zhang, Zhihong
Fang, Shaoming
coredata.dc:description: A novel catalyst comprising the one-dimensional (1D)/two-dimensional (2D) nanostructural nanohybrid of CoCu-based zeolitic imidazolate framework nanosheets (CoCu-ZIF NSs) and graphdiyne (GDY) nanowires (denoted as CoCu-ZIF@GDY) was prepared using the strategy of covalent-organic framework (COF) on metal-organic framework (MOF). The prepared CoCu-ZIF@GDY was further explored as electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and overall water splitting in alkaline solution. The CoCu-ZIF NSs were synthesized by a two-step method, including the preparation of bulk CoCu-ZIF nanoparticles and the formation of CoCu-ZIF NSs by adding excess Co2+ and Cu2+ ions. Different from routine bulk ZIF nanomaterials, the obtained CoCu-ZIF NSs exhibited an ultrathin 2D structure and comprised a large amount of CoCu2O4 spinel. The GDY COF demonstrated a highly-conjugated structure, strong adsorption ability toward oxygen, nanowire shape, and a large amount of carbon dots. All of these features endowed the CoCu-ZIF NSs and GDY nanowires with excellent electrochemical conductivity, fast electron-transport ability, and high number of exposed active sites, further leading to the synergistic effect and the direct binding between CoCu-ZIF and GDY. The CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid further displayed superior OER performance over other transition-metal- and carbon-nanomaterial-based catalysts, giving an extremely low overpotential of 250 mV to acquire a current density of 10 mA cm−2. When used as the electrolyzer catalyst for two-electrode overall water splitting, it also gave a low overpotential of 290 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and outstanding stability possibly due to the ability of the GDY network to prevent CoCu-ZIF from corroding in alkaline solution. Overall, this work provided a promising strategy for the exploration of electrocatalysts based on bimetallic MOFs and COFs for clean energy.

head.ce:title: Efficient electrocatalytic water oxidation by using the hierarchical 1D/2D structural nanohybrid of CoCu-based zeolitic imidazolate framework nanosheets and graphdiyne nanowires

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel catalyst comprising the one-dimensional (1D)/two-dimensional (2D) nanostructural nanohybrid of CoCu-based zeolitic imidazolate framework nanosheets (CoCu-ZIF NSs) and graphdiyne (GDY) nanowires (denoted as CoCu-ZIF@GDY) was prepared using the strategy of covalent-organic framework (COF) on metal-organic framework (MOF). The prepared CoCu-ZIF@GDY was further explored as electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and overall water splitting in alkaline solution. The CoCu-ZIF NSs were synthesized by a two-step method, including the preparation of bulk CoCu-ZIF nanoparticles and the formation of CoCu-ZIF NSs by adding excess Co2+ and Cu2+ ions. Different from routine bulk ZIF nanomaterials, the obtained CoCu-ZIF NSs exhibited an ultrathin 2D structure and comprised a large amount of CoCu2O4 spinel. The GDY COF demonstrated a highly-conjugated structure, strong adsorption ability toward oxygen, nanowire shape, and a large amount of carbon dots. All of these features endowed the CoCu-ZIF NSs and GDY nanowires with excellent electrochemical conductivity, fast electron-transport ability, and high number of exposed active sites, further leading to the synergistic effect and the direct binding between CoCu-ZIF and GDY. The CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid further displayed superior OER performance over other transition-metal- and carbon-nanomaterial-based catalysts, giving an extremely low overpotential of 250 mV to acquire a current density of 10 mA cm−2. When used as the electrolyzer catalyst for two-electrode overall water splitting, it also gave a low overpotential of 290 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and outstanding stability possibly due to the ability of the GDY network to prevent CoCu-ZIF from corroding in alkaline solution. Overall, this work provided a promising strategy for the exploration of electrocatalysts based on bimetallic MOFs and COFs for clean energy.

ce:section.ce:para: Sustainable energy generation from renewable sources is one of the greatest challenges in the scientific world [1–3]. Water electrolysis is considered to be the primary and simplest way for scalable hydrogen production. However, targeted efficiency remains restricted due to the sluggish kinetics and high activation overpotential of anodic water oxidation, i.e., oxygen evolution reaction (OER) [4–6]. Many research efforts have been devoted to identifying highly efficient and robust electrocatalysts. To date, precious metal ruthenium and iridium oxides are the benchmark water-oxidation catalysts, but their low abundance and high cost greatly hinder their commercial-scale applications [7,8]. Thus, highly efficient and robust alternatives to these precious-metal catalysts are important to develop using earth-abundant elements.

Porous organic frameworks (POFs) such as metal-(MOFs) or covalent-organic frameworks (COFs) are a new class of crystalline and microporous materials that are considerably attractive for catalysis [9]. The unique properties, namely, large specific surface area, tunable pore sizes, and perfect nanostructures, of POFs enable their extensive use in gas storage and separation, energy storage, biomedical and biosensing, supercapacitors, energy conversion, and catalysis [10]. The accessible pores and open channels of MOFs can provide accommodation space for electrolytes, which can help in the diffusion of reactants and the transport/evolution of generated oxygen gas [11]. Therefore, MOFs are promising for OER electrocatalysis because of their active metal centers or prefunctionalized organic ligands. However, the shuffled orientations and low conductivity of MOF-based nanomaterials hinder their wide-ranging application. Most MOFs are also unstable in acid or alkaline solution and have poor electrochemical activity [12]. Consequently, directly using MOFs as efficient electrocatalysts for OER remains a big challenge.

Recently, many attempts have been made to develop MOF-derived carbon nitrides and transition-metal compounds for electrocatalysts [13], such as Ni2P/CoN-PCP [14], Ni2P–CoP [15], InOF-16-Ar/O2-T [16], and Fe–Ni–P/rGO [17]. However, the calcination and doping or functionalization using Se, P, or S elements can greatly increase high energy and cost owing to tedious preparation steps. As such, how to develop efficient electrocatalysts based on MOF-derived transition-metal compounds still needs the deep exploration. It demonstrates that converting traditional bulk MOFs crystals into two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets can remarkably improve OER performance [7], which is ascribed to the desirable features of 2D nanomaterials, such as high percentages of exposed active atoms to guarantee high catalytic activity and large nanometer thickness to ensure rapid mass transport and charge transfer [18]. Ni-M-MOF (M = Fe, Al, Co, Mn, Zn, and Cd) nanosheets-[19] and 2D Ni-MOF@Fe-MOF [7] have been synthesized and evaluated as catalysts for water-oxidation catalysis in alkaline medium due to the 2D nanosheet morphology, as well as the synergistic effect between Ni active centers and other metal species. As a subclass of MOFs, zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) are considered as effective electrocatalysts for water splitting [20,21]. Xiao et al. fabricated Co-based bimetallic phosphide ultrathin nanosheets (CoM-P-NS, M = Ni, Mn, Cu, and Zn) by using MOF nanosheets as precursors and produced synergistically active sites for OER owing to the homogeneous composition and unique porous architecture [1]. Liu et al. explored a nanocomposite of CoNi-ZIF embedded by MoS2 nanosheets as a bifunctional electrocatalyst for overall water splitting in alkaline solution, delivering current densities of 10 mA cm−2 at ultralow cell voltages of 1.55 V [22]. Apparently, ZIF-based catalysts can be used to remarkably improve catalyst efficiency.

To date, MOFs and COFs with hierarchical structures such as COFs-on-MOFs, MOFs-on-COFs, and MOFs/COFs have been developed and applied in catalysis [23]. The transformation around MOF crystals generates micro- and mesopores at the MOF/COF interface that provide porosity far superior to the combined MOF and COF components [24]. Diverse nanostructure of core-shell NH2-MIL-68@TPA-COF [25] and PCN-222-Co@TpPa-1 [5] hybrids have been synthesized and used as effective visible-light-driven photocatalyst. These hybrids inherit the merits of high crystallinity, large surface area, outstanding visible-light response, and tunable band gaps of their MOF and COF components [26]. These findings indicate that the combination of COFs and MOFs can endow hybrids with a synergistic effect between the metal centers and COF networks and a hierarchical structure of one-dimensional (1D)/2D, 2D/three-dimensional, as well as enhance the electron transfer, which all lead to the hybrids’ remarkable characteristics. However, the hybridization of MOFs and COFs with different-dimensional nanostructures remains in its initial stage. Indeed, MOF/COF hybrid catalysts with extensive potential applications in the clean-energy and energy-conversion field need to be developed.

As a 2D (single-atom-thick) carbon allotrope, graphdiyne (GDY) is an important kind of COFs synthesized under mild conditions in solution [27] and was widely used in the fields of lithium-ion batteries [28–32], electrocatalysts, and photocatalysts [33–36]. Specifically, the GDY-based composites display excellent electrocatalytic activity for OER or HER, such asGDY-exfoliated and sandwiched Fe/Co layered double-hydroxide nanosheet arrays grown on nickel foam [37], metalloporphyrin-based GDY [38], CoNx@GDY nanosheets [39], and GDY-supported NiFe LDH [40]. All these findings indicate that the close contact between the GDY network and other nanomaterials can enable the integration of various catalysts and the optimization of their electrocatalytic performances. However, no study about the combination of ZIF nanosheets and GDY support has been reported. Considering the outstanding catalytic performance, porous networks, diverse nanostructure, and high electrochemical conductivity of these materials, the development of their composites is highly attractive.

Considering the superior electrocatalytic performances of bimetallic ZIF nanosheets, good electrochemical activity of GDY nanowires, and promising synergistic hybridization effect between 1D and 2D structures, we synthesized herein a novel catalyst comprising COF-on-MOF nanostructures based on GDY nanowires and CoCu-ZIF NSs and evaluated its OER performance in alkaline solution (Scheme 1 ). The CoCu-ZIF NSs were obtained by a two-step method initially involving the addition of excess Co2+ and Cu2+ ions into CoCu-ZIF bulk to produce the NS structure, followed by the formation of a large amount of CoCu2O4 spinel. The innovations of the present work were as follows: (i) the close contact of GDY-based COF with CoCu-ZIF NSs in the absence of any binder can be a promising approach to the exploration of hierarchical 1D/2D COF-on-MOF nanostructure; (ii) the doping of CoCu2O4 spinel within the CoCu-ZIF NSs can greatly improve the electrochemical activity, which has not yet been reported in other studies; and (iii) the prepared CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid showed outstanding OER performance and overall water-splitting ability. Overall, this work provided a new strategy for preparing hierarchical 1D/2D COF-on-MOF structures and broadened the applications of COFs/MOFs in the clean-energy and energy-conversion fields.
The parts of chemicals and reagents, synthesis of the CoCu-ZIF NSs, and basic characterizations of all samples are provided in the S1 of the Supporting Information.

The synthesis of GDY nanowire was referred to the reported literature with a slight modification [41]. Briefly, 1,3,5-triethynylbenzene (30 mg) was dissolved in 40 mL methanol, and 500 μL of NH3·H2O was slowly added to the above solution under the continuous stirring. Subsequently, the solution of CuI (8 mg) in 6 mL of methanol was slowly added to the above mixed solution at 0 °C. The reaction was stirred at room temperature in the dark for 72 h. The obtained product was filtered and washed thoroughly with water and methanol, separately. The yellow production was then dried at 60 °C in an oven. The synthesis scheme of CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid is similar with that of GDY nanowire, except that the addition of 8 mg CoCu-ZIF NSs was mixed with 1,3,5-triethynylbenzene.

OER and HER measurements were performed with an electrochemical station (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Co., Model CHI660E), while Pt wire and an Ag/AgCl (3.5 M KCl solution) electrode were used as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. For the working electrode, 1 mg catalyst was dissolved in 100 μL of nafion and 900 uLof deionized water to form an ink solution. Then 10 μL of the suspension was dropped onto rotating disk electrode (RDE, 3 mm in diameter), and naturally dried at room temperature. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was determined at a sweep rate of 10 mV s−1 with a speed rate of 1600 rpm in 1.0 M KOH. For OER, the LSV curves were measured in O2-saturated KOH solution. The durability and stability of the CoCu-ZIF@GDY for the OER were assessed by chronoamperometry and CV in 1 M KOH at the potential of 1.5 V. The potentials vs. Ag/AgCl were converted a reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) [42]. The electrochemical active surface areas (ECSA) was calculated from the double-layer charging curves using cyclic voltammograms (CVs) at different scan rates of 5–55 mV s−1 in a small potential range from 1.0 to 1.15 V vs. RHE. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in a frequency range of 10−5 to 0.01 Hz with an AC voltage of 5 mV with the same configuration.

Overall water splitting test was carried out using a two-electrode system, where the nickel foam-supported CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid was simultaneously used as the anode and cathode. For the anode or cathode, 1.0 mg catalyst was dispersed in 1.0 mL of Milli-Q water by sonication. Afterwards, the suspension was cast onto the nickel foam followed by drying at room temperature in air. The Pt/C (20%)∥RuO2 group was applied to test the overall water splitting for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological characterizations of CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphologies and nanostrucutres of the GDY nanowires, CoCu-ZIF NSs, and CoCu-ZIF@GDY were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The SEM and TEM images of the as-prepared CoCu-ZIF (Fig. S1) revealed its nanosheet-like structure, along with the observation of CoCu2O4 crystal. As for the GDY framework (Fig. S2), it verifies it is comprised a number of relational twisting and discontinuous nanowires and large amount of nanoparticles, which were embedded within the nanowire walls.

The SEM and TEM images of the CoCu-ZIF@GDY are illustrated in Fig. 1 . As expected, GDY nanowires grew on the surface of CoCu-ZIF NSs and then all stacked together (Fig. 1a). However, the size of CoCu-ZIF@GDY is smaller than that of the CoCu-ZIFs (Fig. 1b), as further confirmed by the TEM image (Fig. 1c). Meanwhile, the TEM images (Fig. 1c and d) illustrate that the CoCu-ZIF still maintains hollow structure, and many GDY nanowires are dispersed on the surface of CoCu-ZIF NSs. The high-resolution TEM image of CuCO-ZIF@GDY shows clear lattice fringes of 0.203 and 0.244 nm attributed to GDY and the (311) plane of CoCu2O4, respectively [43]. Furthermore, Energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping analysis indicates the uniform dispersion of Co, Cu, and C elements in the CuCO-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid (Fig. 1f–h). The associating mass percents for Cu, Co, and C elements were supplied in Table S1. Moreover, Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) characterization was performed to probe the thickness of the CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanosheets. As shown Fig. S3, the height profile reveals that the thickness of the CoCu-ZIF@GDY is about 2.0 nm, suggesting its ultra-thin nanosheet structure. Owing to the strong π−π* stacking between the CoCu-ZIF NSs and GDY nanowires [44,45], it is easy for CoCu-ZIF to combine with GDY nanowires. Additionally, the Mott–Schottky plots of CoCu-ZIF and GDY were measured (Fig. S4), which can be used to distinguish the band structure of one semiconductors. The detail experiment was added in S1.3 (See Supporting Information). The result verifies that both the slope of CoCu-ZIF and GDY were positive, indicating that they were n-type semiconductors. Therefore, it seems that the CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid may form an n-n type heterojunction.

X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed to evaluate the crystalline structures and chemical structures of CoCu-ZIF NSs, GDY, and CoCu-ZIF@GDY hybrid. As shown in Fig. S5a, the XRD pattern of the developed CoCu-ZIF NSs suggests four distinctive diffraction peaks at 10.21°, 19.8°, 34.1°, and 59°, consistent with reported literature [46]. Six characteristic peaks at 15.8°, 22.5°, 23.8°, 25.5°, 38.2°, and 39.4° are observed in the XRD pattern of pure GDY [47]. Peak broadness is due to the relatively small crystal regions of the sample [48]. For the CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid, three peaks belonging to GDY nanowires and CoCu-ZIF NSs are observed.

Fig. S5b shows the FT-IR spectra of the as-prepared CoCu-ZIF, GDY, and CoCu-ZIF@GDY. For the CoCu-ZIF NSs, the intense peak at 1048 and 1377 cm−1 can be assigned to the C–O stretching vibration [49] and umbrella vibration of –CH3 [50]. The peak at 2917 cm−1 is related to C–H vibration from the alkyl chain [49], and the peak at 3441 cm−1 represents an O–H functional group [51]. Additionally, the peak at 872 cm −1 in the GDY spectrum is due to the CH3 rocking mode [52], whereas those at 1561 and 1406 cm−1 are assigned with the CC vibration from graphitic domains and symmetric stretching of free carboxylate groups [53]. Meanwhile, the peak at 3298 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric vibrations of OH groups [54]. For CoCu-ZIF@GDY, the main peaks belong to CoCu-ZIFs and GDY, indicating the successful combination of both samples.

Fig. S5c and d show the Raman spectra and N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of GDY nanowires, CoCu-ZIF NSs, and CoCu-ZIF@GDY. For the Raman spectra of the CoCu-ZIF NSs, the bands centered at ∼462 and 514 cm−1 are attributed to metal-O vibrations in CoCu-ZIF NSs [55], whereas the weak band at 1048 cm−1 is ascribed to the deformation mode of hydroxyl [56]. The peaks at 1369 and 1579 cm−1 are indexed to the D and G bands of sp 2 carbon vibration domains in aromatic rings, respectively [57], while a weak peak recorded at 2193 cm−1 can be ascribed to alkyne-related stretching vibrations. For the CuCo-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid, the D and G bands from GDY can be observed whereas the characteristic peaks attributed to CuCo-ZIF are almost invisible. It is attributed to the growth and accumulation of GDY nanowires on the surface of CuCo-ZIF NSs, which is confirm by SEM and TEM results. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of the GDY, CoCu-ZIF, and CoCu-ZIF@GDY are 170.75, 24.32, and 156.63 m2 g−1, respectively, whereas the corresponding pore volumes are 0.49, 0.14, and 0.74 cm3 g−1, respectively. In contrast to GDY, CoCu-ZIF@GDY has a smaller BET surface area and a larger pore volume because of the reduction of pores and channels caused by the in situ reaction [58].

To evaluate the changes in chemical components and structure before and after the integration of CoCu-ZIF NSs and GDY nanowires into CoCu-ZIF@GDY, their XPS characterizations were performed. As depicted in Fig. S6, the high-resolution Co 2p, Cu 2p, C 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra of CoCu-ZIF NSs were analyzed to verify their chemical structure and environment. The fitted curve of the high-resolution Co 2p XPS spectrum comprises two groups of peaks at the binding energies (BEs) of 781.1 and 796.8 eV, which are attributed to the Co 2p 3/2 and Co 2p 1/2, respectively (Fig. S6a). Four components are fitted out in the Co 2p 3/2 peak, including Co3+ (780.8 eV) and Co2+ (782.5 eV), along with their satellite peaks at BEs of 784.8 and 787.1 eV [59,60]. The peaks at BEs of 796.6, 798.2, 802.9, and 806.6 eV are assigned to Co3+, Co2+, and their satellite peaks of Co 2p 1/2, respectively. Owing to the large peak area of Co3+ compared with all other species, it dominates in the CoCu-ZIF NSs. As regards the high-resolution Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. S6b), the deconvoluted peaks at BEs of 934.6 and 935.7 eV in the high-resolution Cu 2p XPS spectrum can be assigned to the Cuα (five-coordinated) and Cuβ (six-coordinated) of Co2+ of Cu 2p 3/2, respectively [61]. The well-defined shake-up line with the two component peaks at 941.5 and 943.7 eV suggests the feature of divalent Cu for both Cuα and Cuβ. Meanwhile, the peaks at BEs of 954.8 and 962.6 eV are due to the Cu2+ and its satellite part of Cu 2p 1/2, respectively. This finding shows that only Cu2+ species coexists with Co2+ and Co3+ and is coordinated by the N species of the ligand. With regard to the C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S6c), three peaks are identified at 284.7, 285.4, and 288.5 eV, which correspond to the C–C, C–N, and COO groups, respectively. The O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S6d) is composed of two component peaks at the BEs of 531.4 and 532.2 eV, which are characteristic of CO and C–O bonds, respectively. The multicomponent feature in the chemical structure of CoCu-ZIF NSs can provide a number of catalytic site, thereby enhancing catalysis ability.

The C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of the GDY nanowires were analyzed, and results are shown in Fig. S7a. The C 1s spectrum can be divided into five parts, including sp 2 C–C of benzene rings (284.1 eV), sp CC (284.7 eV), C–O (285.2 eV), CO (286.1 eV), and COO (288.6 eV). This finding demonstrates that the area ratio of the sp and sp 2 subpeaks is about 2, confirming that the benzene rings in this GDY sample is linked with one another by –CC–CC−, consistent with the model structure of GDY. The porous structure of the network in GDY endows it with air-adsorption ability, resulting in a clear O 1s signal. The O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S7b) is deconvoluted to the CO, C–O and adsorbed O at BEs of 531.5, 532.2, and 533.3 eV, respectively. All these results confirm that the GDY nanowires comprise –CC–CC−, showing a highly conjugated structure and a large amount O, which tremendously promotes catalysis efficiency.

The high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p, Cu 2p, C 1s, and O 1s contained in the CoCu-ZIF@GDY were also deconvoluted (Fig. 2 ). The Co 2p XPS spectrum of CoCu-ZIF@GDY (Fig. 2a) exhibits the similar analysis results with that of CoCu-ZIF NSs, comprising Co2+ and Co3+ species. By contrast, the Cu 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) shows different XPS deconvolutions. Apart from the Cu2+ component, the peak at the BE of 932.84 eV is assigned to Cu+ of Cu 2p 3/2, whereas the peak at the BE of 952.75 eV is due to Cu+ of Cu 2p 1/2. It hints that the partial Cu2+ ions were reduced to Cu+ in the presence of GDY. Moreover, five parts of sp 2 C (CC), sp C (C–C), C–N, CO, and COO are coexisted in CoCu-ZIF@GDY NSs (Fig. 2c), whereas three components are present in the O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d), including CO, C–O, and adsorbed O. Compared with GDY nanowires, the relative content of the adsorbed O is larger than that of GDY nanowires, indicating their excellent OER electrocatalytic performance [62].

The electrocatalytic activities of OER in 0.1 M KOH solution of the obtained catalysts including GDY, CoCu-ZIF, and CoCu-ZIF@GDY were evaluated using a three-electrode configuration. RDEs modified with the catalysts served as the working electrode, whereas Ag/AgCl and graphite electrode was used as the reference and counter electrodes, respectively. LSV measurements were carried out to obtain the polarization curves of all catalysts, which were then compared with that of a commercial RuO2 catalyst. The OER electrocatalytic activities of the as-prepared catalysts are illustrated in Fig. 3 a and Table S2. The overpotential obtained at 10 mA cm−2 (η10) of the CoCu-ZIF@GDY electrode (vs. RHE) is 1.48 V, which is much smaller than those of GDY (η10 = 1.56 V), CoCu-ZIF (η10 = 1.53 mV), and commercial IrO2 (η10 = 1.50 V). This finding indicates that the OER catalyst of CoCu-ZIF@GDY catalyst outperforms to their individual components, further proving the synergistic effect between GDY and CoCu-ZIF.

The catalytic kinetics of GDY, CoCu-ZIF, and CoCu-ZIF@GDY were further examined through their Tafel slopes, which were deduced from their corresponding LSV curves. As shown in Fig. 3b and Table S2, the Tafel slope of CoCu-ZIF@GDY is as small as 57 mV dec−1, whereas those of GDY and CoCu-ZIF are 75 and 63 mV dec−1, respectively. These results suggest that although GDY and CoCu-ZIF display fast electrocatalytic OER reaction, the integration of GDY and CoCu-ZIF greatly facilitates the electron transfer and boosts the OER reaction. Owing to the strong activation of the O–O bond by binding O2 to the bridge position between Co and Cu atoms, the electrocatalyst containing CoCu2O4 exhibits excellent OER performance [63]. Given the outstanding redox capability, good anticorrosion performance in alkaline solution, intrinsic theoretical conductivity, and multiple oxidation states of the CoCu-based nanostructures, their electrical conductivity and electrochemical activity are higher than those of their single components copper and cobalt [64]. Additionally, the synergistic effect between Co2+ or Cu+ and the alkyne and aryl π-conjugated networks hinders the tendency of CoCu-ZIF NSs to stack. The unique porous structure of GDY also endows the ZIF NSs with a strong adsorption energy [65]. Most importantly, the proposed CoCu-ZIF@GDY catalyst exhibits higher OER performance than GDY-based catalysts and ZIF-derived nanomaterials (Fig. 3c and Table S3), such as Co-GDY/CF (η10 = 270 mV; Tafel slope = 99 mV dec−1) [38], GDY (η10 = 460 mV; Tafel slope = 282 mV dec−1) [66], EG/Co(OH)2/ZIF-67 (η10 = 335 mV; Tafel slope = 63 mV dec−1) [67], CNTs@NiCoP/C (η10 = 297 mV; Tafel slope = 57.3 mV dec−1) [68], and Co9S8/NSCNFs-950 (η10 = 314 mV; Tafel slope = 54 mV dec−1) [69]. All these findings shows that the combination of GDY and CoCu-ZIF can efficiently enhance the OER activity. Turnover frequency (TOF) is a significant indicator used to evaluate electrocatalysts, which can illustrate the intrinsic electrocatalytic behavior [70]. The TOF values for Co and Cu active sites containing in CoCu-ZIF and CoCu-ZIF@GDY at 240 mV in 1.0 M KOH were calculated based on the estimated numbers of active sites reported by Mahmood et al. [71], separately. As displayed in Fig. S8a, the TOF value of CoCu-ZIF@GDY for the Co active site was 1.27 s−1, which is much larger than that of CoCu-ZIF (0.18 s−1). Meanwhile, the TOF value for Cu active site of CoCu-ZIF@GDY (0.028 s−1) was also larger than that of CoCu-ZIF (0.013 s−1) (Fig. S8b). It demonstrates the more active sites containing in CoCu-ZIF@GDY catalyst as compared to those of CoCu-ZIF, thus leading to the superior electrocatalytic performances. The durability and stability of CoCu-ZIF@GDY for the OER were further assessed by chronoamperometry and CV in 1 M KOH solution at a potential of 1.5 V. As shown in Fig. 3d, the LSV of CoCu-ZIF@GDY was recorded for 5000 cycles over the OER potential region, for which no degrade significantly over 5000 cycles is observed. Additionally, CoCu-ZIF@GDY demonstrates good stability, with about 95.1% current retention after 40 h of continuous reactions (inset of Fig. 3d). By contrast, the current retention of commercial IrO2 is only 61.1% of the original after 40 h under the same measurement conditions. Thus, CoCu-ZIF@GDY illustrates superior electrochemical durability toward OER in alkaline medium.

ECSA was calculated based on the electrochemical double-layer capacitance C DL by scanning CVs between 1.1 and 1.2 V vs. RHE [72]. As shown in Fig. S7, CoCu-ZIF@GDY displays a capacitive current larger than those of CoCu-ZIF and GDY. Accordingly, the calculated C DL of CoCu-ZIF@GDY (17.8 mF cm−2) is approximately 14.8 and 1.51 times greater than those of the monocomponent GDY (1.2 mF cm−2) and CoCu-ZIF (11.8 mF cm−2) (Fig. 3e), indicating a substantially enlarged electrochemically active area. As depicted in Fig. 3f, the EIS Nquist plots of three catalysts were tested, and the R ct values were calculated according to the equivalent circuit (inset of Fig. 3f). As summarized in Table S4, CoCu-ZIF@GDY has a smaller R ct (12.6 Ω) than those of GDY (57.7 Ω) and CoCu-ZIF (18.4 Ω). As aforementioned, abundant CoCu2O4 nanoparticles are contained in the CoCu-ZIF NSs, which can greatly enhance electronic conductivity, electrochemical activity, and high stability in alkaline solution owing to the presence of mixed valence metal cations [73]. Additionally, the ultrathin nanosheet structure of CoCu-ZIF is helpful for the electron transfer [74]. Thereby, directly combining the GDY nanowires with CoCu-ZIF nanosheets without any binder led to good mechanical adhesion and electrical contact, which decreases the contact resistance and charge-transfer resistance and thus further accelerate the reaction kinetics [66]. Both C dl and EIS measurements further confirm that CoCu-ZIF@GDY demonstrates higher electronic activity than GDY and CoCu-ZIF, which can substantially improve the electron transfer and enhance the electrocatalytic kinetics toward OER.

The above findings clearly indicate that CoCu-ZIF@GDY with its unique hybrid nanostructure and multiple components possesses outstanding OER performance, which is attributed to the following reasons. First, the synergistic effect between Co and Cu species and GDY would markedly enhance the electrocatalytic ability. Consequently, the surface high-valence-state Co2+ and Cu2+ in the CoCu2O4 and CoCu-ZIF network is easily oxidized. Furthermore, the CoCu-ZIF and GDY components concurrently act as conductive carriers, which may remarkably promote OER owing to the effective electron pathway of the active metal oxo/hydroxide sites [68]. Second, the intrinsically superior activity toward OER of the CoCu-ZIF NSs ensures the outstanding overall water-splitting ability. Third, the porous structure of the CoCu-ZIF@GDY composite provides numerous channels and active sites for boosting electrolyte diffusion, electron transport, and bubble release, thereby enabling rapid mass/charge transport during OER. All these findings indicate that CoCu-ZIF@GDY’s performance for OER is superior to the individual components due to the synergistic effect.

The HER performance of CoCu-ZIF@GDY in alkaline solution was also investigated. As displayed in Fig. S9a, the LSV curves of GDY, CoCu-ZIF, and CoCu-ZIF@GDY for HER performance were obtained and compared with that of the benchmark Pt/C catalyst. The results reveal that the onset potential at the current density of 1 mA cm−2 (η0) and η10 of the CoCu-ZIF@GDY catalyst for HER were 354.4 and 446.5 mV (Table S5), respectively. This finding suggests that η0 and η10 values of CoCu-ZIF@GDY catalyst are larger than that Pt/C (η0 = 36.9 and η10 = 94.7 mV), but smaller than those of GDY (η0 = 375.4 and η10 = 498.4 mV) and CoCu-ZIF (η0 = 364.6 and η10 = 454.2 mV). It hints that the electrocatalytic performance of the CoCu-ZIF@GDY catalyst is superior to those of GDY and CoCu-ZIF. Concurrently, the Tafel slopes of GDY, CoCu-ZIF, and CoCu-ZIF@GDY catalysts are 124, 135, and 88 mV dec−1, respectively. Although the HER performance of CoCu-ZIF@GDY is not very excellent, it is comparable to those of some transition-metal catalysts obtained by pyrolysis (Table S6).

In view of the potential HER and outstanding OER performance of CoCu-ZIF@GDY, it was used to assemble an alkaline electrolyzer to serve as both anode and cathode in 1.0 M KOH. For comparison, the performances of GDY, CoCu-ZIF and commercial Pt/C (20%)∥RuO2 were also examined (Fig. 4 a). These results suggest that CoCu-ZIF@GDY electrode has the best water-splitting activity among all catalysts. The CV curves (with iR correction) illustrate that CoCu-ZIF@GDY electrode gives a small cell voltage of 290 mV to deliver 10 mA cm−2, which is much greater than those of GDY (410 mV), CoCu-ZIF (350 mV), and Pt/C (20%)||RuO2 couple (340 mV), as well as other reported state-of-the-art electrodes comprising transition metals (Table S7 and Fig. 4b). Meanwhile, the overall water splitting experiments were also measured in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1.0 M PBS, respectively. Unfortunately, the cell voltage to deliver the current density of 10 mA cm−2 was as large as 660 mV in 0.5 M H2SO4. The current density less than 10 mA cm−2 in the test voltage range was observed in 1.0 M PBS. These results indicate that the as-prepared CoCu-ZIF@GDY can only be used as the electrocatalyst for overall water splitting in alkaline medium.

Long-term stability is an important factor for evaluating the practicability of electrocatalysts. Fig. 4c depicts the chronopotentiometric curves of CoCu-ZIF@GDY recorded in 1.0 M KOH solution at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. V−t measurements hint an almost steady potential, showing only a very slight decline in current density (1.7%) compared with the initial level. Conversely, the Pt/C (20%)||IrO2 couple exhibits a substantial decrease (21.1%) after 40 h of continuous water splitting, indicating the high stability of CoCu-ZIF@GDY electrode for overall water splitting.

The nanostructure and crystallinity of CoCu-ZIF@GDY were also characterized to examine the changes that occurred after the overall water-splitting stability tests as anode for 40 h. No substantial change is observed in the XRD pattern (Fig. 5 a) of CoCu-ZIF@GDY compared with the fresh one. However, the SEM image (Fig. 5b) displays some porous or cracks within the CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanohybrid, which is absent in that of fresh electrocatalyst. Thus, some layers and/or morphological structures of CoCu-ZIF@GDY were damaged due to the stress encountered during stability tests, although the homogeneous element distribution of Cu, Co, and C is retained (Fig. 5c–e). The corresponding mass percent for Cu, Co, and C elements were supplied in Table S8. This result is corroborated by the slight changes in the high-resolution Co 2p and Cu 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 5f and g). Although there is no substantial change in the XPS spectra of Co and Cu elements, the Co 2p 3/2 peaks for the hybrids shifted to a lower binding energy as compared with the original results. The slight shift observed in the Co 2p peak could be attributed to the possible formation of small CoOx domains during the prolonged water oxidation cycle [75]. All these results hint that CoCu-ZIF@GDY retains high catalytic performance companied with its stable chemical structure.

We prepared a 1D/2D structural nanohybrid of ultrathin CoCu-ZIF NSs combined with GDY nanowires and explored their efficiency as electrocatalyst for OER and overall water splitting. The metal-ion mixtures of Co2+/Co3+ and Cu2+/Cu+, active CoCu2O4 spinel, ultrathin NS-like nanostructure, and large specific surface area endowed the CoCu-ZIF NSs with high electrochemical activity and abundant catalytic sites. Moreover, the 1D GDY nanowires exhibited a high-conjugate structure and comprised large amounts of carbon dots. Owing to the synergistic effect between the carbon framework of GDY and the active catalyst metal sites of CoCu-ZIF NSs, the OER performance of CoCu-ZIF@GDY greatly outperformed the individual components CoCu-ZIF and GDY, as well as other previously reported MOF-based composites or derivatives. The 1D/2D CoCu-ZIF@GDY nanostructure also gave an extremely low overpotential of 1.48 V at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a cell voltage of 1.52 V at 10 mV cm−2 for overall water splitting in a two-electrode alkaline electrolyzer. Overall, the 1D/2D CoCu-ZIF@GDY electrocatalysts can provide a new strategy to explore MOFs or COFs for clean-energy applications.
